result.markdown:
--- title: "Characteristics of Adelie, Chinstrap, and Gentoo Penguins" subtitle: "INFO 526 - Summer 2026 - Final Project" author: "Michelle Chow" title-slide-attributes: data-background-image: images/watercolour_sys02_img34_teacup-ocean.jpg data-background-size: stretch data-background-opacity: "0.7" data-slide-number: 1 format: revealjs: theme: sky center: false vertical-align: top editor: visual execute: echo: false --- ## Motivation - Fascination for penguins - Global warming impacting penguin colonies - Essential to track characteristics of penguins that can impact colony health ## Data ::: row ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
species island bill_len bill_dep flipper_len body_mass sex year
Adelie Torgersen 39.1 18.7 181 3750 male 2007
Adelie Torgersen 39.5 17.4 186 3800 female 2007
Adelie Torgersen 40.3 18.0 195 3250 female 2007
Adelie Torgersen NA NA NA NA NA 2007
Adelie Torgersen 36.7 19.3 193 3450 female 2007
Adelie Torgersen 39.3 20.6 190 3650 male 2007
````` ::: ::: ::: ::: {style="font-size: 0.7em;"} :::{.incremental} - Palmer Penguins dataset - First created in TidyTuesday in July 2020 - Updated in 2025 - Contains 8 columns and 344 rows of data - 3 species penguins - 8 different penguin characteristics - 3 islands - Focus on island, species, year and body mass variables ::: ::: ## Questions :::{.incremental} 1. How does the island where Adelie, Gentoo, and Chinstrap penguins reside impact their bill length? 2. How has the mean body mass of the Adelie, Gentoo, and Chinstrap penguins changed between 2007 and 2009 and does the island impact the mass? ::: # Visualizations ## Visualizations - Question 1 {.top} ::: {style="text-align:center;"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=1800} ::: ::: ::: ::: {style="font-size: 0.6em; text-align:center;"} :::{.incremental} - Only Adelie penguins inhabit all islands - Gentoo penguins have the largest penguin population on Biscoe - Chinstrap penguins live on Dream - Adelie penguins had similar number of observations as Gentoo penguins (\~120) ::: ::: ## Visualizations - Question 1 ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=1800} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {style="font-size: 0.7em; text-align:center;"} :::{.incremental} - Chinstrap had the longest median bill length - Adelie had the shortest median bill length - Gentoo outlier with a bill length of 60mm - Similar Adelie penguin bill lengths across 3 islands - Biscoe had the least variation - Chinstrap has the largest variation in bill length compared to all species ::: ::: ::: ::::: ## Visualizations - Question 1 ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){width=1800} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="25%"} ::: {style="font-size: 0.5em;"} :::{.incremental} All species: - Most chinstrap penguins have a bill length of 51mm - Moderate amount of Chinstrap penguins with a bill length of 41mm - Gentoo penguins have a bill length of 45mm to 50mm - Right-skewed - Adelie penguins have a bill length of 37mm to 41mm ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="25%"} ::: {style="font-size: 0.5em;"} :::{.incremental} Specific to Adelie penguins: - Biscoe and Torgersen penguins tend to have longer bill lengths - Distribution of Torgersen bill lengths more symmetrical - Biscoe penguins are right-skewed - Dream penguins are left-skewed ::: ::: ::: ::::: ## Visualizations - Question 2 ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=1800} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {style="font-size: 0.7em; text-align:center;"} :::{.incremental} - Gentoo penguins have the highest mean body mass (\~5100g) - Adelie and Chinstrap penguins have lower mean body mass (\~3700g) - +50g net change to Gentoo penguins mean body mass - -50g net change to Adelie penguins mean body mass - Chinstrap penguins mean body mass remains unchanged ::: ::: ::: ::::: ## Visualizations - Question 2 ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](presentation_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=1800} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {style="font-size: 0.7em; text-align:center;"} :::{.incremental} - Torgersen Adelie penguins mean body mass decreased drastically (\~250g drop) - May be cause for concern - Biscoe Adelie penguins mean body mass increased drastically (\~250g increase) - Dream Adelie penguins remains mostly unchanged ::: ::: ::: ::::: # Conclusion ## Conclusion - Question 1 ::: {style="font-size: 0.7em"} *All species* - Chinstrap penguins correlated with longer bills but have more variation in bill length - Adelie penguins correlated with shorter bills and have the least variation in bill length *Adelie penguins* - Most symmetrical distribution for Torgersen Adelie penguins - Dream Adelie penguins correlated with shorter bill lengths than the other 2 islands ::: ## Conclusion - Question 2 ::: {style="font-size: 0.7em"} *All species* - Gentoo penguins have the highest mean body mass (\~5100g) - Adelie and Chinstrap penguins had similar mean body mass (\~3700g) - Gentoo penguin had net positive change in mean body mass - Adelie penguin had net negative change in mean body mass - Chinstrap penguin mean body mass remains unchanged *Adelie penguins* - Biscoe penguins net increase in mean body mass between 2007 and 2009 (3625g to 3850g) - Dream penguins net decrease in mean body mass between 2007 and 2009 (3675g to 3650g) - Torgersen penguins net decrease in mean body mass between 2007 and 2009 (3755g to less than 3500g) ::: ## Images ![Image credit: Danielle Navarro, Percolate.](images/watercolour_sys02_img34_teacup-ocean.png){fig-align="center" width="500"}

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
